preFix.title: Castrating male piglets
preFix.subtitle: The legal window, the method, and what to watch afterwards.
preFix.excerpt: Boar taint in uncastrated male pigs is a real risk at slaughter weight if they are processed after puberty. UK law allows the keeper to use a rubber ring on piglets under 7 days old, without anaesthetic. The procedure, the recovery signs, and the records you need.
preFix.sourceNotes: Welfare of Farmed Animals (England) Regulations 2007 and Schedule 8 (pig husbandry operations). DEFRA/APHA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs (2003, updated guidance 2021). Merck Veterinary Manual, swine castration. AHDB Pork, piglet management guide.

content.text: Castrating male pigs bred for pork removes the risk of
at slaughter. The UK permits the keeper (not only a vet) to castrate piglets under 7 days old using an
without anaesthetic, under Schedule 8 of the Welfare of Farmed Animals Regulations 2007. Piglets over 7 days old must be castrated surgically by a veterinary surgeon under anaesthetic.

attrs.body: The legal window for keeper-performed castration closes at the end of day 7. A piglet that is 8 days old cannot be legally castrated by the keeper using any method. The only option after that point is surgical castration by a vet under anaesthetic. If you are unsure of the exact birth date, treat the litter as potentially over-age and contact your vet.
attrs.title: The 7-day rule is absolute

content.text: Day 3 to 5 is the preferred window. The piglet is old enough to have received adequate first-milk intake and to be past the first 48 hours, when health challenges are most likely. It is also young enough that the scrotal tissue is small and the ring technique is straightforward. Do not castrate on day 1 or 2; the piglet needs time to establish nursing.

Only castrate male piglets you intend to raise for pork. Male pigs kept for breeding (boars) are not castrated. Check each male for both testes: hold the piglet upright and look at the scrotal area. Both testes should be visible as two distinct lumps. A
with one or both testes not descended cannot be ringed by the keeper; note the piglet and discuss with your vet.

Load a rubber ring onto the four prongs of the rubber ring castrator, stretching it wide. Hold the piglet firmly around the body with the hind end upward, testes visible. Pass the stretched ring over both testes together, positioning it at the base of the scrotum, between the testes and the body wall. Confirm both testes are below the ring, then release the ring from the prongs. The ring should sit tight at the scrotal neck.
Return the piglet to the sow immediately. Piglets reunited with the litter quickly settle back to nursing.

The ring causes progressive ischaemia (loss of blood supply) to the scrotal tissue. The piglet will show discomfort for a few hours, typically moving with a slightly stiff gait. Most return to normal nursing behaviour within half a day. The scrotal tissue will discolour and dry over the following 2 to 3 weeks and drop off cleanly.

Check the area daily for the first week. A small amount of swelling around the ring site is normal. Warm, wet, foul-smelling swelling indicates infection and needs a vet call. The skin should remain dry and the tissue should gradually darken and shrink.
Record the date, litter identification, number of piglets castrated, and the method in your holding register. UK pig keepers must maintain medicine and husbandry records by law.

items.fix: Reapply a new ring immediately if the piglet is still within the legal window. Do not attempt to reuse the original ring once it has been released; it will not grip correctly.
items.cause: Ring was not seated correctly at the scrotal neck, or the prongs were released before the ring was properly positioned
items.symptom: Ring has slipped off after fitting

items.fix: Call your vet. A piglet that will not nurse after 12 hours is at risk of hypoglycaemia; it may need supplementary feeding or pain relief administered by the vet.
items.cause: Pain response, or a piglet that was already unwell before castration
items.symptom: Piglet very dull and not nursing 12 hours after castration

items.fix: Contact your vet. Tetanus is a risk with any infection near a ring site in livestock. The vet will advise on treatment and whether a tetanus antitoxin injection is needed.
items.cause: Contaminated bedding, or the ring was not placed correctly
